coredata.dc:description: Food allergy is increasingly prevalent and poses notable health risks, which underscores the urgent need to develop reliable and sensitive detection methods for effective identification of food allergens. This study aims to address the limitations of existing methods by developing an immunoassay utilizing bacteriophage/carbon dots (CDs)@silica core-shell nanospheres. Two CDs with different emission wavelengths (513 nm for Green CDs, 645 nm for Red CDs) were synthesized for signal development and amplification. The nanobodies (Nbs) displayed on M13 bacteriophage were employed for the rapid fluorescence quantification of peanut allergen Ara h 3 and macadamia allergen Mac i 1 through magnetic separation. Generally, the method was established with detection limits of 9.5 and 10.2 ng/mL for Ara h 3 and Mac i 1, respectively, demonstrating a sensitivity of 2–5 times greater than traditional methods. Collectively, this multiplexed testing offers a potential analytical strategy based on bacteriophage for effective screening of food allergens.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of M13 bacteriophage/Carbon quantum dots@silica core-shell nanospheres fluorescent sensor. (a) Preparation process of G-CDs and R-CDs. (b) Preparation of MBs@Nbs. (c) Preparation of phages-CDs@SiO2. (d) Methodology of developed fluorescence immunosensors.
Structural characterization of CDs and CDs@SiO2. TEM images of (a) G-CDs, (b) R-CDs, (c) GCD@SiO2, and (d) RCD@SiO2. (e) FT-IR spectra of G-CDs and GCD@SiO2. (f) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and RCD@SiO2.

Optical properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2. (a) UV–Vis absorption (purple), fluorescence excitation (λex, blue) and emission (λem, green) spectra of G-CDs in aqueous solution; The inset: The photographs of G-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (b) UV–Vis absorption (purple), fluorescence excitation (λex, blue) and emission (λem, red) spectra of R-CDs in aqueous solution; The inset: The photographs of R-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (c) Fluorescence excitation (λex, blue) and emission (λem, orange) spectra of GCD@SiO2 in aqueous solution. The purple and green curves represent the excitation and emission spectra after GCD@SiO2 loading onto the M13 phage (phage-GCD@SiO2), respectively. (d) Fluorescence excitation (λex, green) and emission (λem, purple) spectra of RCD@SiO2 in aqueous solution. The blue and red curves represent the excitation and emission spectra after RCD@SiO2 loading onto the M13 phage (phage-RCD@SiO2), respectively. (e) Fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs and GCD@SiO2. (f) Fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs and RCD@SiO2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Construction of fluorescence immunosensor and optimization of conditions. (a) Schematic diagram of a fluorescent immunosensor. (b) Zeta potential of MBs, MBs@Nb43, and MBs@Nb139. (c) Optimization of the added concentration of Nbs coupled MBs in preparation of capture probe. (d) Zeta potential of CDs, CDs@SiO2, and Phage-CDs@SiO2. (e) Optimization of the added concentration of Phages in preparation of signal probe. (f) Optimization of the utilized amount of capture probes. (g) Optimization of time of allergen capture. (h) Optimization of the added amount of detection probes. (i) Optimization of the incubation time. Data were expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). Each test was biologically repeated three times at least.

The detection of Ara h 3 and Mac i l by M13 bacteriophage/Carbon quantum dots@silica core-shell nanospheres mediated fluorescent detection. (a) Calibration curve of the developed Ara h 3 immunoassay was determined after non-linear fitting. (b) Linear standard of the established Ara h 3 fluorescence immunoassay. (c) Calibration curve of the developed Mac i l immunoassay was determined after non-linear fitting. (d) Linear standard of the established Mac i l fluorescence immunoassay. (e) Fluorescence intensity at 524 nm and 654 nm in test system with varying concentrations (15, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000 ng/mL) of Ara h 3 and Mac i l. (f) Specificity and cross-reactivity of fluorescence immunoassay. Data were expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). Each test was biologically repeated three times at least.
Recoveries of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 in different samples by the Fluorescent immunoassay method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was μg/mL.
Each assay was repeated three times; the result for recovery was the average of three replicates.
RSD was the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean.

head.ce:title: M13 bacteriophage based fluorescence immunoassay against food allergens of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food allergy is increasingly prevalent and poses notable health risks, which underscores the urgent need to develop reliable and sensitive detection methods for effective identification of food allergens. This study aims to address the limitations of existing methods by developing an immunoassay utilizing bacteriophage/carbon dots (CDs)@silica core-shell nanospheres. Two CDs with different emission wavelengths (513 nm for Green CDs, 645 nm for Red CDs) were synthesized for signal development and amplification. The nanobodies (Nbs) displayed on M13 bacteriophage were employed for the rapid fluorescence quantification of peanut allergen Ara h 3 and macadamia allergen Mac i 1 through magnetic separation. Generally, the method was established with detection limits of 9.5 and 10.2 ng/mL for Ara h 3 and Mac i 1, respectively, demonstrating a sensitivity of 2–5 times greater than traditional methods. Collectively, this multiplexed testing offers a potential analytical strategy based on bacteriophage for effective screening of food allergens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food allergy is a hypersensitivity condition mediated by food allergen-specific immunoglobulin E (IgE), which can lead to systemic symptoms ranging from mild urticaria to potentially fatal reactions. The prevalence of food allergies has been increasing in recent years, affecting individuals across all age groups, from children to adults (Barni et al., 2020; Sicherer & Sampson, 2018). Currently, there are no effective immune therapeutics available in clinical practice, and avoidance of food allergens remains the primary strategy for individuals with sensitivities. According to the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the main allergens are proteins from eight foods, including eggs, milk, and tree nuts, with sesame seeds identified as the ninth (Seth et al., 2020). Various food allergens can potentially be classified into similar families, exhibiting analogous epitopes and contributing to cross-reactivity in detection methods (Chinthrajah et al., 2015). The multiplex contamination of food allergens during production, storage, transportation, or through fraudulent practices necessitates the simultaneous detection of different food allergens to provide comprehensive information in a single operation (Campuzano et al., 2020; Prado et al., 2016). The legal requirements for food allergen labeling established by the FDA and the European Union underscore the need for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of potential food allergens in food products (Taylor & Hefle, 2006).

Immunoassays have been extensively researched and utilized for the detection of trace amounts of food allergens due to their high sensitivity, relatively good specificity, and simplicity (Filep et al., 2022; Hu, Lin, et al., 2023). Conventional antibodies, including monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) and polyclonal antibodies (pAbs), have frequently been employed in the development of immunoassays; however, they may exhibit potential cross-reactivity with different allergens (Shi et al., 2017). We have reported the preparation of the antibody fragment of nanobodies (Nbs) through phage display technology for immunoassay development against food allergens to indicate the potential contamination, and demonstrated their superiority of higher specificity and easier manipulation compared with conventional antibodies (Hu et al., 2022; Li, Nie, et al., 2024).

M13 bacteriophage is a type of filamentous virus measuring approximately 880 nm by 6.5 nm. It serves as a multifunctional scaffold comprising 2700 copies of the pVIII protein and 3 to 5 copies of the pIII protein at both ends. The pIII protein is primarily responsible for displaying peptides or proteins bound to the target on the surface, utilizing phage display technology. In contrast, pVIII proteins are mainly involved in the transduction or amplification of biological and chemical modifications (Huang et al., 2019; Jaroszewicz et al., 2022). M13 phage particles are regarded as effective agents for constructing immunoassays based on surface-displayed Nbs for the capture and detection of trace food allergens, offering enhanced selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the collection of amplified phages with displayed Nbs can streamline the process by eliminating cumbersome steps associated with Nb conjugation and purification. Consequently, M13 phage particles are advantageous for customizable detection in the development of multifunctional biosensors (Fang et al., 2021; Lee, Lee, Lee et al., 2023; Zhan et al., 2022). The use of phage display technology to fuse specific Nbs to the surface and load nanomaterials should be an interesting immunosensor.

Fluorescence immunoassay has become a common method for allergen detection due to its simple operation, high sensitivity, and multi-target detection (Wang et al., 2020). As a promising carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for their straightforward synthesis, low toxicity, excellent fluorescence properties, broad excitation spectrum, ease of functionalization, and good biocompatibility (Moradi-Kalbolandi et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2019). Until now, the emission wavelengths of CDs have been concentrated in the blue region, which can cause background interference in many food matrices (Li, Nie, et al., 2024). Additionally, encapsulating the CDs in a silica shell provides stable, permanent surface chemical modifications to avoid the challenges inherent in directly modifying the surface of the CDs, and the CDs are enriched within the shell in anticipation of a stronger fluorescence signal output (Fu et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2016). The simultaneous detection of different food allergens based on CDs decorated with M13 phages is expected to provide multiple signals from the varied emissions of different CDs.

The frequent daily intake of peanuts or peanut derivatives increases the likelihood of peanut allergen-related allergic diseases in infants, young children, and adolescents (Sicherer et al., 2010). To date, more than 20 peanut allergens have been identified, with Ara h 3 recognized as a significant allergen, accounting for approximately 25 % of the total protein in peanuts and affecting about 50 % of patients with peanut allergies (Rabjohn et al., 1999; Singh et al., 2016). This allergic reaction occurs due to the immune system mistakenly identifying these proteins as harmful invaders, triggering a cascade of immune responses. Notably, these allergens share similarities with certain other nuts, which is why individuals allergic to peanuts may also be sensitive to other tree nuts. Macadamias are gaining considerable attention due to their unique qualities and nutritional properties, as well as their rising popularity worldwide, may pose cross-reactivity risks for individuals allergic to peanuts (Crespo et al., 2007; Hu et al., 2021). Therefore, it is critical to analyze them simultaneously based on the reported Nbs specific to Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 (Hu et al., 2021; Hu, Zhang, et al., 2023).

In this study, functionalized M13 phages were utilized as a biological scaffold, while green and red water-soluble CDs were synthesized. The CDs were encapsulated into silica spheres (CDs@SiO2) and covalently attached to M13 phages to create immunofluorescent probes. To facilitate rapid, precise, and straightforward allergen detection, Nb-functionalized magnetic beads (MBs@Nbs) were employed for magnetic separation to capture Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 from food samples. The CDs@SiO2-labeled functionalized M13 bacteriophage (phage-CDs@SiO2) served as a fluorescent nanoprobe to monitor changes in fluorescence signals at various emission wavelengths, thereby enabling the simultaneous immunodetection of the peanut allergen Ara h 3 and the macadamia allergen Mac i 1 in food samples (Fig. 1 ).

The Ara h 3 standard was obtained from Indoor Biotechnologies, Inc. (Charlottesville, Virginia, USA). β-lactoglobulin (BLG), bovine serum albumin (BSA), chicken ovalbumin (OVA), κ-casein, citric acid, TEOS, APTES, glutaraldehyde, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl] carbodiimide (EDC), and polyethylene glycol (PEG6000) were sourced from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin and alpha-methyl mannopyranoside were procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carboxylated magnetic spheres were acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Anti-Ara h 3 Nb P43, Anti-Mac i 1 Nb M139, and the VCSM13 helper phage were obtained from laboratory collection. All reagents utilized in this study were of analytical grade unless otherwise specified.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was conducted using the FEI Tecnai G2 F30 (FEI Company, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed with a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted using the Thermo Escalab 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). UV–vis spectra were recorded with the AquaMate 8000 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Cary Eclipse fluorometer and a Tecan SPARK® multimode microplate reader (Tecan Group Ltd., Männedorf, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and purification of Mac i 1
ce:section.ce:para: The crude protein extract was prepared following the procedures detailed in our previous study. Subsequently, the macadamia allergen Mac i 1 was purified from the macadamia protein extract using a well-established method with specific modifications (Kabasser et al., 2022). In general, 5 mg of crude protein extract was dissolved in 20 mL of extraction reagent (0.2 M Na2HPO4, 0.1 M citric acid, 0.2 M NaCl, pH 4.8) while stirring at 4 °C for 30 min. The precipitates were collected by centrifugation at 12,000◊g for 10 min. The supernatant was then neutralized to a pH of 7.4 in preparation for dialysis into the binding buffer (20 mM Tris-HCl, 0.5 M NaCl, 1 mM MnCl2, 1 mM CaCl2, pH 7.4). The protein solution was subsequently applied to a 5 mL HiTrap ConA Sepharose-4B column (Cytiva, Marlborough, MA, USA) for further purification, and Mac i 1 was eluted in a single fraction using the elution buffer (0.5 M alpha-methyl mannopyranoside, 20 mM Tris-HCl, 0.5 M NaCl, pH 7.4). After dialysis in PBS buffer containing 0.5 M NaCl, the eluate underwent additional purification on a Superose 6 Increase 10/300 GL column (Cytiva, Marlborough, MA, USA). Protein purity was assessed using SDS-PAGE, and the identity of the band corresponding to Mac i 1 was further validated by ELISA according to the protocols outlined in our previous report (Hu et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production and characterization of Nb-displayed phages

ce:section.ce:para: The pMECS phagemids containing Mac i 1 and Ara h 3 Nb encoding genes fused to the phage pIII protein-encoding gene were transformed into E. coli TG1 competent cells. Single colonies were picked and inoculated into 5 mL of LB medium for overnight culturing. VCSM13 helper phages were added to infect E. coli TG1, enabling the rescue of phage particles encapsulated with the recombinant pMECS phagemid. The pIII protein fused with Nbs automatically assembled for the release and production of M13 phages displaying these Nbs on their surface. Phages in the culture supernatant were separated from E. coli by centrifugation and precipitated by adding 1/10 (v/v) of PEG6000/NaCl (200 g PEG6000, 146.1 g NaCl, brought to 1 L with H2O) to the obtained supernatant. The phages were pelleted after centrifugation and resuspended in 1 mL of sterile PBS. The concentration of the resulting phages was determined, and they were stored at −80 °C for further use. The antigen binding specificity and affinity of the phages were assessed by ELISA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding specificity and cross-reactivity of phages
ce:section.ce:para: After immobilizing the target protein on the solid matrix of the microtiter plate, the phages were evaluated by ELISA to assess their binding specificity and affinity for the antigen, as well as cross-reactivity with unrelated antigenic proteins. ELISA plates were typically coated with Ara h 3, Mac i 1, or irrelevant proteins (BLG, κ-casein, OVA, BSA) at a concentration of 10 μg/mL. Following a blocking step with 3 % skim milk powder, phages were added and incubated for 1 h to allow potential interactions with Ara h 3, Mac i 1, or the unrelated proteins. Mouse anti-His IgG (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and HRP-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgGs (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) were incubated sequentially. TMB substrate was then added for signal development, and the response at 450 nm was recorded using a microplate reader.

The affinity of the phages was assessed using a saturated ELISA with immobilized antigen, typically coated with 10 μg/mL of Mac i 1 or Ara h 3 on a microtiter plate. Following a blocking step with 3 % skim milk powder, phages were introduced at serial dilutions of 1012, 1011, 1010, 109, 108, 107, 106, 105, and 104 pfu/mL. After washing with PBST (PBS containing 0.5 % Tween-20), mouse anti-his IgG and HRP-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgGs were added in sequence. TMB substrate was subsequently applied for signal development, and the optical density at 450 nm was measured. The apparent affinity of the phage was defined as the concentration of phages that produced half of the maximum signal.

Green CDs (G-CDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with phenol and ethylene diamine as precursors (Yang et al., 2019). Specifically, 235 mg of phenol and 0.15 mL of ethylene diamine were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the supernatant was subsequently subjected to dialysis using a dialysis membrane for 24 h, and the synthesized CDs were stored at 4 °C for future use.

For the synthesis of red CDs (R-CDs), meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin (0.0395 g) was used as the aromatic precursor, ethylenediamine (300 μL) as the nitrogen source, and citric acid (0.0480 g) as the carbon source. The prepared homogeneous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 14 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particle residues, resulting in the synthesis of porphyrin-red CDs (Wu et al., 2020). Unreacted reagents were removed by dialysis, and the R-CDs were stored at 4 °C until further use.

Fluorescent silicon spheres were prepared by following the Stöber method with certain modifications (Yang et al., 2020). Briefly, 1 mL of G-CDs or R-CDs stock solution, 0.5 mL of ammonia, and 0.2 mL of TEOS were sequentially added to 50 mL of ethanol under magnetic stirring. After 2 h of incubation, the solution became cloudy, and a white precipitate appeared. Subsequently, 2 mL of APTES was added, and stirring continued for an additional 4 h. Finally, the pellet was separated from the solution by centrifugation at 10,000 ◊ g for 15 min, and the pellet was rinsed three times with ethanol and water, respectively. The obtained nanoparticles were stored for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of phage-CDs@SiO2 fluorescent nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Glutaraldehyde was used as a cross-linking agent to couple the synthesized CDs@SiO2 with phages, following the procedure described by F.Y. Yu and F.S. Chu, with certain modifications (Fig. 1c) (Yu & Chu, 1996). In general, phages were diluted to 1012 pfu/mL in 0.01 M PBS. To this solution, 100 μL of CDs@SiO2 and 100 μL of 50 % glutaraldehyde were added, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 3 h. The reaction was terminated by adding 2 mg of glycine to the reaction mix, which was stirred for an additional 30 min. The conjugates were then collected by centrifugation at 10,000 ◊ g for 15 min and rinsed sequentially with deionized water. Finally, the conjugates were resuspended in 1 mL of PBS and stored at 4 °C until further use.

The covalent coupling of MBs and Nbs was achieved using NHS/EDC (Fig. 1b) (Han et al., 2020). Typically, MBs at a concentration of 10 mg/mL were dissolved in a 2-morpholino-ethanesulfonic acid solution (0.1 M, pH = 5.5). Subsequently, 80 μL of EDC (10 mg/mL) and 40 μL of NHS (10 mg/mL) were added and incubated for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of the MBs. The MBs were collected using a magnetic stand to remove any residual EDC and NHS and then resuspended in a PBS solution containing 50 μg of Nbs to facilitate the reaction between the carboxyl and amino groups. The MBs conjugates were then separated from the unbound Nbs using a magnetic stand and washed twice with PBST. The conjugates were blocked with a 3 % BSA solution to conceal any remaining binding sites on the surface of the MBs. After washing with PBST, the Nb-decorated MBs were resuspended in 300 μL of PBS (containing 0.5 % BSA) and stored at 4 °C for immediate use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of food allergens with phage-CDs@SiO2 mediated fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: Two core-shell fluorescent CDs with different emission wavelengths were used to evaluate the simultaneous detection of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 in food, utilizing Nb-displayed phages for specific and sensitive identification of the corresponding food allergen proteins (Fig. 1d). Briefly, the food allergen proteins were captured and enriched by incubating MBs@Nbs in the food matrix. Then, phage-CDs@SiO2 probes were added to the mixture to facilitate interaction with the MBs@Nbs and allergen complex during the subsequent incubation. The acquired magnetic conjugates were magnetically separated from the unbound phage-CDs@SiO2 probes, and a further washing process with PBST ensured the removal of nonspecific binding. The obtained beads were then resuspended in 100 μL PBS for signal acquirment. The wavelength and fluorescence were detected using a Tecan SPARK® multimode microplate reader. The cutoff value was determined by analyzing blank samples (n = 10). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the developed immunoassays were calculated according to the “Eurachem Guide” (Magnusson, 2014), which defines LOD and LOQ as the concentration corresponding to the mean of the blank values plus threefold or tenfold standard deviation (SD), respectively (Hu et al., 2022).

To improve the sensitivity of phage-CDs@SiO2-based detection, a series of optimizations-including the concentrations of MBs@Nbs and phage-CDs@SiO2, as well as incubation time and usage-were finalized before establishing a standard curve by following the steps described in the detection procedures above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of food allergen proteins in foods
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of the established fluorescence immunoassay, food samples were prepared from commercially available skim milk, oatmeal, confectionery, chocolate, and cake, each supplemented with the corresponding food allergens. Samples were prepared according to established methods reported in the literature (He et al., 2017). Briefly, solid oatmeal, confectionery, chocolate, and cake were ground into a powder and dissolved in an extraction buffer (20 mM Tris-HCl, 2 % Tween-20, pH 8.0) at a ratio of 1:20 (m/v). The corresponding food allergens were then added to the samples for further analysis. The mixture was stirred overnight at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 ×g. Skim milk was diluted 5000-fold prior to the trial. The recoveries of the spiked samples were calculated following the aforementioned steps for fluorescent detection using MBs@Nbs and phage-CDs@SiO2 probes. Each analysis was performed in triplicate, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to assess the replicability of the developed immunoassay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of macadamia allergen of Mac i 1
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the accuracy and detection of the developed immunoassay, the crude protein extract of macadamia nuts was used for further purification of Mac i 1. The protein of interest was initially enriched through precipitation and resuspension. Subsequently, Mac i 1 was purified via Con A affinity chromatography to capture glycosylated proteins. The purified content was evaluated using SDS-PAGE, where a dominant band corresponding to approximately 50 kDa was visualized, showing a purity greater than 95 % (Fig. S1a). It has been reported that Mac i 1 Nb of Nb68 can specifically bind to the target protein (Hu et al., 2021). Therefore, an ELISA was performed to assess the binding of the selected Nb to the purified Mac i 1. The results indicated that the Mac i 1-specific Nb efficiently binds to the purified protein, confirming the identity of the obtained protein as Mac i 1 (Fig. S1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Nb-displayed phages
ce:section.ce:para: The encoding genes of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 specific Nbs were inserted into the phagemid pMECS to enable the fused expression of Nbs with the pIII protein. This approach facilitated the rescue of recombinant phages using VCSM13 helper phages for the encapsulation of modified pMECS phagemids and the packaging of Nb-pIII fusions. Overall, the yield of Nb-displayed phages was determined as 8◊1012 pfu per liter of culturing medium, as assessed through phage plaque analysis.

It is essential to maintain the complete binding capacity of phage display Nbs to target allergen proteins. Therefore, the binding specificity and affinity of Nb-displayed phages have been evaluated and confirmed through ELISA assessment. As illustrated in Fig. S2a and 2b, the binding of Nb-displayed phages to the corresponding allergens was validated by a significant response to the immobilized Ara h 3 and Mac i 1, respectively. Furthermore, the cross-reactivity of the acquired phages was assessed by incubating them with irrelevant allergens, including BLG, κ-casein, OVA, and BSA. The results indicated that no significant interaction between the acquired phages and the irrelevant allergens was observed, which supports the complete binding capacity and specificity of Nb-displayed phages.

For the evaluation of the binding affinity of Nb-displayed phages, a saturation ELISA was conducted to determine the apparent affinity. Specifically, the allergen proteins Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 were immobilized on microtiter plates, and phages at varying concentrations were incubated to facilitate interaction with the immobilized allergens. As illustrated in Fig. S2c and 2d, the concentration of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 specific phages corresponding to half the maximum signal were determined to be 109.4 ± 100.4 and 109.2 ± 100.3 pfu/mL, respectively. Following normalization, the relative apparent affinities were calculated to be 0.0041 and 0.0026 pM, respectively. If surface plasmon resonance (SPR) were employed, the measured affinity would likely be more sensitive (Shivangani Lohia et al., 2022). This fully demonstrates the high binding properties of the Nb-displayed phage, likely attributable to the inherent flexibility of M13, which allows for twisting to adjust the ligand configuration and enhance adsorption to the target protein (Li, Chen, et al., 2024). These findings sufficiently illustrate the applicability of these phages for immunoassay development.

The solvothermal method was applied for the synthesis of CDs (Fig. 1a). The morphology and particle size of the CDs were characterized using HRTEM, and the images showed that the CDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed. The average particle size of G-CDs was determined to be 2.33 ± 0.32 nm (Fig. 2a and Fig. S3a), while R-CDs had an average size of 2.6 ± 0.55 nm (Fig. 2b and Fig. S3b). Following the Stöber method, a large number of CDs were encapsulated in a silicon shell construct to facilitate surface decoration with amino groups, resulting in CDs@SiO2 with good dispersion. The average particle sizes were 120 ± 8.67 nm for GCD@SiO2 (Fig. 2c and Fig. S3c) and 127 ± 13.2 nm for RCD@SiO2 (Fig. 2d and Fig. S3d), respectively.

FT-IR and XPS were used to study the composition and surface chemistry of CDs. Fig. S3e and S3i illustrate the XPS full spectra of CDs. Peaks observed at 284.8, 400 and 531 eV correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s. XPS analysis indicated that CDs were rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, with atomic percentages of 66.17 %, 12.35 %, 21.48 % for one sample, and 72.45 %, 13.24 %, and 14.3 % for another. High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S3f and S3j) exhibited four peaks at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively. For G-CDs, the high-resolution scans of the N1s region (Fig. S3g) revealed two prominent peaks centered at 399.4 and 401.5 eV, associated with CN and NH, respectively. For R-CDs, the N1s spectrum in Fig. S3k could be differentiated into three types of nitrogen at 398.0, 400.0, and 401.5 eV, associated with CN, CN, and C-NH2, indicating the presence of pyridine nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and alkyl ammonium. As shown in Fig. S3h and S3i, the O1s spectrum can be divided into two peaks; the peak at 531.2 eV corresponds to CO, while the peak at 532.5 eV corresponds to CO. The FT-IR measurements were used to characterize the surface structure of CDs and CDs@SiO2. As shown in Fig. 2e, the band at 1125 cm−1 represents CO stretching vibrations, and the absorptions at 1295 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic tensile vibrations of CN and the bending vibrations of OH, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 1679 cm−1 were attributed to CC and CO. The characteristic absorption band at 3127 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. For GCD@SiO2, the peaks around 1095 and 793 cm−1 are attributable to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si and the bending vibrations of the SiO groups, respectively, indicating the presence of TEOS. The tensile and bending vibrations attributed to NH in the bands around 3442 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 also confirm the presence of APTES. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 2f, the tensile vibrations of CC (1590 cm−1), CC (1485 cm−1), and CN (1327 cm−1) indicate that R-CDs have a conjugated aromatic structure. Additionally, the tensile vibration peaks of CO (1157 cm−1), CO (1644 cm−1) and O-H/N-H groups (3357 cm−1) indicate the presence of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs. These groups contribute to the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs in aqueous solutions (Shen et al., 2017). The characteristic peaks of CDs@SiO2 (1600 cm−1 for the bending vibration of NH, 3300–3470 cm−1 for the stretching vibration of NH, and 793 cm−1 for the bending vibration of SiO and the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si) indicate that the surface of CDs was successfully coated with silica.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the prepared CDs and CDs@SiO2 were studied using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs displayed a clear peak at 404 nm, which was associated with strong emission. This strong emission may be attributed to the capture of excited state energy by the surface states (Li et al., 2017). When excited at 410 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of G-CDs in aqueous solution was observed at 513 nm. Similarly, the fluorescence performance of R-CDs was characterized (Fig. 3b), with a clear peak in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 413 nm and a maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 645 nm when excited at 415 nm. These results were consistent with the UV absorption spectrum and indicated that the red shift in emission was caused by the surface structure and states.

Using polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon chemicals as precursors or expanding the sp2 domain size of CDs was an effective method for achieving redshift emission photoluminescence. Meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin, with its conjugated aromatic structure, reduces the energy gap by increasing the size of the sp2 domain, Additionally, the nitrogen atom, as an electron donor, can bond with a carbon atom, disrupting the structure of the carbon ring. This disruption creates a new energy gap between the excited and ground states through radiative recombination. Consequently, nitrogen doping can narrow the band gap and produce strong red fluorescence (Xu et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2019).

As shown in Fig. S4a and 4b, the normalized fluorescence spectra of G-CDs (360–430 nm) and R-CDs (390–430 nm) at different excitation wavelengths were determined. When the excitation wavelength was changed, the fluorescence emission peak of the CDs remained essentially unchanged. To facilitate their application in immunoassay development, the CDs were encapsulated in silica shells for enhanced stability and functionality (Guo et al., 2018). Silica-coated CDs exhibited significant optical properties in aqueous dispersion. As shown in Fig. 3c, GCD@SiO2 had a maximum emission at 518 nm when excited at 415 nm, while RCD@SiO2 had a maximum emission at 648 nm when excited at 416 nm (Fig. 3d). The normalized fluorescence spectra of GCD@SiO2 (360–430 nm) and RCD@SiO2 (390–430 nm) at different excitation wavelengths were illustrated in Fig. S4c and S4d. The emission spectra of GCD@SiO2 displayed a slight redshift with increasing excitation wavelength, whereas the emission wavelength of RCD@SiO2 remained unchanged with varying excitation wavelengths.

Next, we measured the emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 under different pH conditions. The results showed that CDs@SiO2 exhibited high emission in the pH range of 5 to 12 (Fig. S4e-h), with the maximum effect observed at pH 8. Therefore, pH 8 was chosen as the optimal condition for further experiments. Thus, pH 8 was chosen as the optimal pH for further experiments. Protected by a silica matrix, CDs@SiO2 demonstrated excellent photostability. As shown in Fig. 3e and f, the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 8.22 ns to 4 ns after the generation of GCD@SiO2, and from 7.69 ns to 2.64 ns after the generation of RCD@SiO2. These results indicate that CDs@SiO2 accelerated the radiative attenuation of fluorescent CDs, ultimately enhancing fluorescence emission (Yang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2014). After 30 days of storage at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Fig. S4i for G-CDs, Fig. S4j for R-CDs) and CDs@SiO2 (Fig. S4k for GCD@SiO2, Fig. S4l for RCD@SiO2) did not decrease significantly, thus ensuring reliability for subsequent immunodetection.

M13 phage serves as a functional biological carrier by fusing the pIII region with Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 specific Nbs on its surface using phage display technology. The protein in the pVIII region is covalently bound to the ammoniated CDs@SiO2 through the action of glutaraldehyde. When the allergen is present in the solution, the allergen MBs@Nbs is captured by antigen-antibody affinity, while the free M13 phage further recognizes the allergen through affinity, forming a “sandwich” structure. The bound phage is loaded with numerous CDs@SiO2, causing the “sandwich” to emit a sensitive fluorescent signal. The SiO2 shell is coated with a large number of CDs, which further amplifies our detection signal. During this process, a certain amount of time is required for the fluorescence intensity to increase. Once the reaction system of the allergen and the M13 phage reaches equilibrium, the fluorescence intensity does not continue to enhance and may even decrease due to excessive aggregation, leading to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the parameters significantly associated with the performance of the developed fluorescence immunoassay have been characterized to ensure relatively optimized detection (Fig. 4a).

For the preparation of the capturing probe of MBs@Nbs, the successful decoration of Nbs on the employed MBs was initially verified using a zeta assay. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the coupling of Nbs on MBs significantly increases the zeta potential, indicating the dehydration condensation of carboxyl groups with amino groups and confirming the successful conjugation of Nbs to the surface of MBs. Subsequently, the optimal concentration of Nbs was determined for efficient coupling with 100 μL of MBs. The residual Nbs in the supernatant was quantified using the BCA Protein Quantification Kit. As shown in Fig. 4c, adequate conjugation of Nbs on MBs was achieved at a concentration of 50 μg of Nbs. Therefore, this amount has been validated for the preparation of Nb-coupled MBs.

For the preparation of the detection probe, the successful synthesis of CDs@SiO2 was verified using the zeta method (Fig. 4d). The surface of CDs@SiO2 was modified with amino groups to enhance the zeta potential. The coupling of phages results in a reduction of amino groups. Furthermore, the excitation and emission spectra of the complex exhibited a redshift of approximately 5 nm (Fig. 3c and d), which may be attributed to the increased size of the particles (Ding et al., 2023). The optimal concentration of phages for effective coupling on CDs@SiO2 was determined by assessing the saturation reaction at varying phage concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4e, the relative concentration of 1012 pfu/mL provided sufficient coupling on CDs@SiO2.

For the successful establishment of this dual-fluorescence immunoassay, the conditions, including the number of capturing probes and detection probes, as well as the capturing and incubation durations, have been validated. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum and stabilized when the volume of capturing probes was increased to 20 μL. The optimized capturing time was depicted in Fig. 4g, with 30 min selected as the optimal duration to ensure sufficient capture of the target allergen protein. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent immune complex stabilized when the signal probes were also administered at 20 μL (Fig. 4h), with an optimal incubation time of 40 min identified (Fig. 4i).

Under optimal detection conditions, a fluorescence immunoassay was developed to simultaneously detect a standard mix of solutions containing Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 at concentrations of 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 4000, 8000, 10,000 ng/mL, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5e, at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm (with emission wavelengths of 524 nm for Ara h 3 and 654 nm for Mac i 1), the presence of the target was positively correlated with the fluorescence intensity of the immunoassay. The corresponding fluorescence intensities of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 gradually increased with increasing target concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 5a-d, a linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity of the signal probes and the logarithm of the concentrations of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1. For Ara h 3, the fitting equation y = 17765logx-18,179 (R2 = 0.9908, n = 3) was obtained, demonstrating a linear range between 22.1 and 2000 ng/mL. For Mac i 1, the fitting equation y = 20353logx-20,753 (R2 = 0.9859, n = 3) was established, with a linear range between 23.1 and 2000 ng/mL. The LOD of this fluorescence immunoassay for Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 was determined to be 9.5 ng/mL and 10.2 ng/mL, respectively. Additionally, a traditional M13-based ELISA assay (Fig. S5a-d) was performed using the optimized parameters, yielding a detection range of 50–3000 ng/mL and an LOD of 19.3 ng/mL for Ara h 3, along with a detection range of 100–4000 ng/mL and an LOD of 33 ng/mL for Mac i 1. Compared to traditional ELISA, the fluorescence immunosensor demonstrated higher sensitivity (2–5 times greater) (Table S1). Furthermore, the designed sensor does not require traditional plate washing, and the entire detection process takes approximately 1 h using magnetic mounts for magnetic bead adsorption, greatly simplifying the operational steps and reducing the detection time (approximately 2 h for traditional ELISA based on phages, compared to 3 h for traditional ELISA). Moreover, this immunosensor offers advantages such as lower cost and the avoidance of the need for specialized instruments for mass spectrometry or biosensor-based detection. In summary, this method not only enables the simultaneous detection of two allergens but also enhances detection sensitivity.

The specificity of the fluorescence detection method was subsequently evaluated, with commonly utilized proteins selected for this assessment. The specificity of the immunoassay was determined by comparing the fluorescence changes induced by unrelated proteins. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, the supplementation of either Ara h 3 or Mac i 1, whether individually or together, resulted in a fluorescence response at the corresponding wavelength. No interference or interaction with other irrelevant proteins was observed, indicating the high specificity of this immunoassay. The proposed fluorescence immunoassay is suitable for the single detection of Ara h 3 or Mac i 1, as well as for the simultaneous detection of both allergens.

To evaluate the practical applicability of this method, commercial samples (skim milk, oatmeal, candy, chocolate, and cake) were selected for recovery analysis by spiking with different concentrations of Ara h 3 or Mac i 1 (0, 0.5, 1, and 1.5 μg/mL). These samples were processed and analyzed using a fluorescent immunoassay method. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rates for spiked Ara h 3 ranged from 86.30 % ± 7.43 % to 104.36 % ± 4.52 %, with an RSD below 9.14 %. The recovery rates for spiked Mac i 1 ranged from 86.75 % ± 7.61 % to 104.92 % ± 4.88 %, with an RSD of less than 7.61 %. These results demonstrate the repeatability and applicability of the established fluorescent immunoassay for the detection of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 in food samples. In comparison to existing reports (Table S1), the detection method presented in this study offers a more promising solution for allergen monitoring.

We have successfully developed a dual-fluorescence immune-sensing system utilizing synthesized green and red CDs for functionalized CDs@SiO2. The Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 Nbs were displayed on the surface of M13 phages to serve as the detection moiety through coupling with CDs@SiO2. By capturing food allergens with Nb-decorated MBs, the fluorescent signal generated allows for rapid quantitative detection of the target proteins. The fluorescence immunoassay proposed in this study enables the simultaneous detection of Ara h 3 and Mac i 1 in food, with a wide linear range of detection. Furthermore, this immunoassay demonstrates the use of Nb-displayed phages for simultaneous detection of food allergens, offering research perspectives on high-throughput analysis of additional allergens in food by incorporating more CDs at different wavelengths. In comparison to traditional ELISA, the designed fluorescence immunosensing system offers enhanced sensitivity and reduced detection time. The method exhibits good specificity and is not interfered with by irrelevant proteins. Additionally, the use of food allergen-specific Nbs provides unique characteristics that address the limitations of conventional antibodies. Importantly, the proposed fluorescent immunosensing platform can be easily adapted to detect other targets by fusing the expression of Nbs to the pIII or pVIII protein of the M13 phage. Overall, this research offers valuable evidence for multitarget detection in food safety surveillance, clinical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.

Yi Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sihao Wu: Investigation. Haitao Wang: Methodology. Xufang Huang: Investigation. Xuemeng Ji: Software. Huan Lv: Methodology. Jing Wu: Methodology. Jingmin Liu: Methodology. Serge Muyldermans: Methodology, Investigation. Yaozhong Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shuo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.
